data.title: BGT: Floating Rate Loans CEF Under Pressure
data.description:
Thesis

We last covered the BlackRock Floating Rate Income Trust (NYSE:BGT) a year ago right before the first Fed rate cut in 2024. In that article we downgraded the CEF to 'Hold', articulating that we were witnessing the start of an easing cycle where floating rate assets will start being pressured. The fund has had a pedestrian performance since.

With a number of visible defaults occurring in the leveraged loan space and jump to default pricing, we are going to revisit this CEF and highlight why we believe this name to be unappealing on a 12-months forward.

A leveraged loan CEF

BGT is a fixed income closed end fund that invests in a portfolio of leveraged loans. Some of its analytics are as follows:

  • AUM: $330 mm
  • Duration: 0.24 years
  • Average portfolio loan coupon: 7.6%
  • Leverage: 15%
  • Premium to NAV: 2%
  • Number of holdings: 505

The fund holds over 92% of its portfolio in leveraged loans, with the rest split between equity slices and high yield bonds. The fund is overweight single-B credits which make up over 55% of the holdings:

  • BBB: 5.5%
  • BB: 28%
  • B: 55%
  • Not rated: 10%
  • CCC: residual

The CEF has seen a reduction in its leverage ratio since we last covered it, but it still runs leveraged risk in the current format.

Say goodbye to high historic recoveries

One of the main characteristics of leveraged loans is the first lien status in the capital structure. This status ensures the loans have a high recovery rate, which historically has been in the 60% to 70% range. However, today's environment is more and more characterized by sudden jump to defaults and low recoveries:

First Brands Group LLC and its affiliates filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection on September 28, 2025, representing one of the largest corporate bankruptcies this year. What is particular for First Brands is not the default itself, but the performance of its liabilities. As we can see from the above graph courtesy of Bloomberg, the leveraged loan from First Brands moved from roughly mid 90s to less than 20 c/$ in a matter of days. This is unheard of almost, with most term loans moving to the 60s or 50s in a bankruptcy. The speed itself is also shocking.

This occurrence tells us that underneath a very calm surface the fundamentals for many businesses are much, much worse than one can think of, and a restructuring can result in a virtual wipe-out for everybody involved in a business.

This simply translates to potential large moves lower in NAV for BGT if we do get a recession. The above bankruptcies have shown us that recoveries are much lower this cycle, and a leveraged fund like BGT could see large gap-downs in NAV if several credits from its portfolio go under.

Credit spreads are too low

Although they have widened out a bit as of late, credit spreads for single-B credits are still very low:

We are using here the ICE BofA Single-B US High Yield Index OAS given its visibility over LSTA data for single-B lev loans spreads. As we can notice from the above chart we are at the bottom of the historic range, with spreads at 320 bps. We have not seen these levels since 2017/2018.

Low credit spreads simply mean the market will very likely move lower in the next leg (i.e. wider spreads). While spreads can stay low, as we saw in April 2025, they can move up quite fast. In fact, BGT had the following drawdown from spreads this year:

As the market experienced a leg down in April 2025, the CEF had a -12% drawdown. Although the fund has a low duration when it comes to interest rates, its spread duration is close to 5 years. Expect similar drawdowns in the future if the market experiences another sell-off.

Still at a premium

The CEF is surprisingly still at a premium, whereas many of its peers are trading at discounts:

We are surprised to see the CEF still trade at a premium to net asset value, when its peers are trading at deep discounts. If we look at the Invesco Senior Income Trust (VVR) and Nuveen Floating Rate Income Fund (JFR) we can see discounts of close to -10%. We are of the opinion that BGT will follow shortly in terms of moving to a discount to NAV. There is pressure to come from this valuation metric in the fund.

Rates are heading lower which means less yield

BGT holds floating rate loans, which means they are subject to SOFR rates and implicitly Fed Funds. As SOFR moves lower, the fund's cashflows are going to decrease, thus moving yields lower.

The Fed cut rates on September 17, 2025, and currently the SOFR futures market is pricing in 100 bps more in cuts by the end of 2026:

The above graph plots the futures market for 1-month SOFR and the Fed Dot Plot. While the 'Dot Plot' is subject to debate, change, and political nominations, the SOFR futures market is not. The market is showing us deep cuts to come in the next year, with 1-month SOFR at 3% by December 2026.

Lower SOFR by 100 bps means BGT's yields will need to be adjusted lower by 1% since that cash-flow will no longer be there to support distributions. While floating rate assets are appealing when rates move higher, they are less so when SOFR moves lower.

Conclusion

BGT is a leveraged loans CEF. The name runs a 15% gearing on top, and represents a take on credit and SOFR. With credit spreads very low and interest rates heading lower, the fundamentals do not look good for the CEF. Surprisingly, the name is still trading at a premium to NAV while its peers are close to -10% discounts. We are of the opinion that BGT will move lower in price in the next 12 months, especially during the next market downturn, which will see much lower recoveries on loans versus historic norms.

data.title: KeyCorp: Strong Results Defy Private Credit Fears
data.description:
Shares of KeyCorp (NYSE:KEY) have been a mixed performer over the past year, losing about 3% of their value. Alongside much of the sector, shares have been steadily declining since August, as concerns about credit quality and the economic outlook weigh on sentiment. Even solid results were not enough to shake these fears, with KEY shares declining ~6% on Thursday. I last covered KeyCorp in July when I reiterated the stock a “Buy,” but this call has not played out, with KEY losing about 9%. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit KeyCorp. I remain bullish.

In the company’s third quarter, KeyCorp earned $0.41 per share, which beat estimates by $0.03 as adjusted revenue grew 17% to $1.9 billion. We continue to see the bank delivering on its promise to expand margins, and while fears are surging around private credit issues, Key’s credit quality is actually improving. In my view, the company’s turnaround is progressing as planned.

Balance sheet composition improvement continues

Average deposits rose 2% from Q2 and nearly 2% from last year. Its total deposit cost declined by 2bps to 1.97%. This is down 42bps from a year ago, safely within the 35%-45% range that I like to see. I was also encouraged to see that its noninterest-bearing balances rose by 2% and were stable as a share of deposits at 19%. After a period of sustained pressure industrywide, it does appear that balances at KEY have bottomed, which will alleviate a source of pressure on margins. I expect deposit costs to decline at least 10bps in Q4, given Fed rate cuts.

On the other side of its balance sheet, average loans rose by $500 billion from Q2 and are flat with last year. Within this, we have seen a meaningful shift within its loan book, which is why loan yields have been fairly resilient despite Fed rate cuts. In particular, it has been allowing its low-yielding consumer mortgages to run off, including a $300 million sequential in Q3. At the same time, it has also been strategically reducing its commercial real estate (“CRE”) exposure, alongside much of the industry. KEY has ample lending capacity with a loan-to-deposit ratio of just 71%. I would expect this to gradually ramp towards 75%, a tailwind for earnings.

It is important to note that its average consumer borrower carries a 766 FICO score, which is extremely strong. With its focus on lending to prime customers, I view this loan book as fairly insulated from labor market softness. Additionally, even with its middle-market focus, 55% of its C&I loans are to issuers with an investment-grade credit rating. That should help mitigate fears about corporate credit emanating from recent challenges in the private credit sphere, which have hit Jefferies (JEF), Zions (ZION), and others. In fact, I would note that credit metrics are improving, which I will discuss further below.

Reinvestment is boosting margins

While the Scotiabank purchase enabled a significant rebalance of its securities portfolio, Key did not fully overhaul it, leaving some capital capacity for growth. As a result, it still has a $1.9 billion loss on its balance sheet, which will gradually roll down, though I do not expect material improvement until 2027, barring a more aggressive Fed rate cutting cycle. The securities portfolio stands at $47.5 billion, and it yields 3.75%, up from 2.98% a year ago. I expect KEY to largely hold the size of the portfolio flat, and we should see ongoing increases in the yield through next year.

Aside from its fixed income securities, KEY has also hedged out some interest rate risk via swaps, which limited its upside from Fed rate increases. This year the average swap rate was about 3.1%, but next year, it rises to 3.5% on ~$27 billion of assets. This will provide a mechanistic uplift to NIM, of about 6bps. By 2027, the headwind from its low-rate fixed swaps should be entirely behind the company.

Given these dynamics, net interest income rose 4% sequentially as its net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded 9bps to 2.75%. NIM is up dramatically from 2.17% a year ago as the repositioning of its securities portfolio has significantly enhanced yields. As low yielding securities and swaps continue to roll off and loan growth continues, there should be further incremental upside to NIM steadily over the 18 months.

Additionally, noninterest income rose by 8% to $702 million. Investment banking fees rose 7.6% to $184 million. We have seen capital markets loosen meaningfully, which has boosted debt issuance. Additionally, there has been an uptick in dealmaking as tariff fears have retreated and antitrust scrutiny is perceived to have lessened. Fee revenue was also boosted by strength in wealth management, with AUM rising 11% to $68 billion thanks to fund inflows and rising markets.

Credit quality is solid

Turning to credit quality, net charge-offs were stable sequentially at 42bps; this was meaningfully better than the 58bps of a year ago. Nonperforming assets were down 6% sequentially and stand at 63bps of loans. This is a meaningful improvement from last year, as well. We have also seen 30-89 delinquencies tick down 3bps to just 24bps since Q1. Across most metrics, we are seeing credit quality improving, even as fears of a recession persist. This speaks to Key’s disciplined underwriting as well as its pivot away from CRE, which was a source of greater credit challenges for the company.

Against this, it is carrying about $1.7 billion of allowances. Key provisioned $107 million for credit losses, down by $31 million from Q2. It has held allowances broadly flat over the past year, even as NPAs have declined. As a result, it has 2.6x coverage of NPAs. I view levels above 2.5x as very strong, and I do not expect material reserve increases from here. Again, while problems may be building private credit, I view Key’s focus on lending to businesses it has a deposit and treasury relationship with as helping to insulate the company from challenges.

Capital is excellent

Additionally, it has one of the strongest balance sheets in the sector, built to withstand unforeseen losses. Key has a published common equity tier 1 (CET1) capital ratio of 11.8%, which is extremely high. Including unrealized losses in AOCI, Key has a 10.3% CET1 ratio, up 170bps from last year, as the capital infusion from Scotiabank immediately strengthened the balance sheet and enabled a material reallocation of its low-yielding investment securities portfolio. I view ~9.5% as a sound level for Key to operate. So, the company has ample capacity to meaningfully grow its asset base and loan book on its existing capital base, which is a material avenue through which it will improve returns and earnings. Given its capital posture, I view its 4.6% dividend yield as secure.

Key is attractive

Overall, I also view management’s update to guidance as positive. Key reiterated its targeted 2% loan growth at the end of the year, but it now sees NII running at the high end of prior guidance, up 22%. It expects NIM to end the year at 2.75%-2.80%, from 2.75% previously. This improved guidance is more consistent with my existing view. Beyond Q4, management is targeting a 15+% return on tangible equity over “the next few years,” up from 12.5% in Q3. Ongoing NIM expansion is a key part of this strategy, and NIM should reach 3% by late next year, in my view.

In August, I expected KEY to exit this year with about $1.80 per share in run-rate earnings power as it continues to deploy capital from its Scotiabank infusion and normalize its balance sheet. This drove my $1.80-$1.90 EPS estimate for 2026. I still see this on track, and if anything, I see modest upside. With shares at 9x 2026 earnings and a peer-leading capital position, I view Key as one of the best and safest value opportunities in the sector. I expect shares to migrate towards $19, and I would use this weakness to "Buy."

data.title: BigBear.ai: Defense AI Play, Tsecond And SMX Partnerships Driving Growth
data.description:
Thesis

BigBear.ai (NYSE:BBAI) shares have seen some very nice momentum this year. There have been quite a few global conflicts which has just added tailwinds to small defense stocks that are adding their own defense solution to on-the-ground conflicts. A particular area of interest for me is any defense company actively trying to push AI into its existing offerings. It's a trend we've seen with quite a few companies this year, and their stock prices have seen some nice upside. BBAI is no different in this aspect. Their recent partnership with Tsecond has opened up a completely new offering: at the edge defense analytics. BBAI's ConductorOS AI analytics system needed a way to be more applicable on the battlefield, with hints of jamming capabilities growing on the ground in the Ukraine war. Which is why having an at the edge hardware partner in Tsecond.ai is so valuable moving forward, as I'll explain. The near-term tailwinds for BBAI also have a new aspect to them now, with the company making a push to offer a Navy-based AI solution. At UNITAS 2025, we saw a partnership announced with SMX to target AI at the edge solutions for unmanned vessels. The stock is up over 100% on the year-to-date chart, as are many other small defense names. Even though the stock has run up quite a lot in recent times, I see a lot more room to run here with the latest tailwinds behind BBAI's new target areas.

2Q25 Review

BBAI 2Q25 results are a bit of a mixed bag. Right now, the company is in a pretty transitional phase financially. We’re seeing some weakness in its core operations being offset by a dramatic capital buffer and nice tailwinds in the defense area, but more importantly, in biometrics. On the operational side, revenue declined about 18% year over year to $32.5 million, down from $39.8 million. But as many investors already know, this was due largely to disruptions in U.S. Army-related programs. Gross margins also compressed, now at 25.0% versus 27.8% in the prior year. So we’re seeing some tighter cost absorption and possibly lower leverage in fixed costs. The adjusted EBITDA loss widened to –$8.5 million compared to –$3.7 million at the same time last year. It saw reduced gross profit and increased investment in R&D. The net loss was also pretty hefty at $228.6 million, though the bulk of that was non-cash. It included a $135.8 million negative revaluation of derivative liabilities tied to convertible debt and warrants. It also included a $70.6 million goodwill impairment. If we exclude those, the underlying operating loss before those accounting charges was roughly $90 million.

Now, if we turn our attention to the balance sheet, it’s much better viewing for investors. BBAI is in a much stronger position than in the past. The company ended the 2Q25 with a company record cash balance of $390.8 million. This was mainly funded by a very successful at-the-market equity raise of about $293 million. If we compare this to just $50 million at the end of 2024, BBAI is in a much better position on a cash basis. Total assets also rose to $599 million from just $344 million at the end of 2024. However, liabilities declined a bit from about $347 million to $333 million. Most importantly, the company managed to shift the shareholders’ deficit to positive equity of $266.6 million. Staying on liabilities, derivative liabilities remain a large line item at $193 million in current liabilities alone. We can put this down mainly to complex convertible instruments. Their debt burden, I thought, was quite manageable, with current debt staying modest, and long-term debt declining from $135.4 million to $102.7 million. For guidance, management actually lowered its 2025 revenue outlook to $125 million to $140 million. The prior target was $160 to 180 million. They also withdrew their adjusted EBITDA guidance, mentioning again the uncertainty in Army contracts.

A recent partnership with Tsecond

This week we saw BBAI and Tsecond come to a nice partnership deal to help bump up BBAI’s ability in at the edge computing. At the edge would refer to computing/hardware that processes data close to where it is generated, so for BBAI’s tech, that would literally be the battlefield. So rather than sending all the data back to a central server or even to the cloud for processing, it gets processed in the field. Tsecond is a great fit for this kind of tech; they make hardware and systems that let the military run AI and analytics at the site of data collection.

The partnership is a pretty important one and should expand BBAI’s relevance and tech presence in edge computing for defense and national security. This segment within defense, albeit only small, is one of the fastest-growing areas of military AI. The collaboration is tipped to have BBAI’s ConductorOS orchestration platform, which deploys and scales AI models across networks, integrated with Tsecond’s BRYCK platform, a portable system that combines petabyte-scale storage. Think of BBAI’s part as providing the conductor in an orchestra; it’s going to coordinate all the AI models, algorithms, and data pipelines to work seamlessly. On Tsecond’s side, it's going to provide the hardware powerhouse. It's a whopping petabyte-scale storage, so huge datasets like video feeds, especially from satellite imagery, or sensor data, will be imputed into about 1,600 TOPS of AI compute. All this is done in a compact, 14-pound device, so something a military squad can carry around. I included a picture of the BRYCK device below for context.

The biggest drivers and tailwinds for many small defense stocks are their ability to push AI computing closer to the battlefield. Many companies have made that push this year, and it's paying off for their stocks. Just look at a company like BlackSky (BKSY), they included AI automation into their satellite surveillance tasks with their Gen-3 satellite and have seen a big bump up in contract numbers. Their stock is also up 179% year to date. So seeing BBAI make this partnership should be a great sign for investors in terms of seeing BBAI stay ahead of the game. I also see this partnership as addressing a critical military challenge here. Operating in battlefield environments is what you call denied, degraded, intermittent, or limited connectivity environments. So current cloud-based AI systems struggle to function effectively when sending data to and from the field. That's why, at the edge, solutions present such an upside with all the jamming/anti-jamming tech getting pushed into the military.

Traditionally, we’ve seen BBAI solely as a software and analytics vendor, so now they have a solid partnership platform to offer hardware-integrated AI infrastructure. This could see their contract value expand, since the military would be getting more applications and products, which would create recurring revenue opportunities. It's worth touching on the applications somewhat here. Think of a drone that is equipped with a BRYCK device. Now, those drone operators can analyze live video feeds in real-time using ConductorOS. So anything from detecting movement, identifying vehicles, or spotting threats instantly, without sending data back to a distant server.

I also see this collaboration as helping to mitigate BBAI’s dependency on delayed U.S. Army programs. Tsecond brings with it new procurement pathways and joint bids with Tsecond for tactical AI deployments. The new deal also came at a great time and fits in nicely with the recent defense funding initiatives, such as the One Big Beautiful Bill. In the bill, over $300 billion was set aside for AI and digital transformation within defense and homeland security. So, within the battlefield space, BBAI has a new offering, and in terms of homeland security, we’re seeing their biometrics veriScan tech gain a lot of momentum across airports in the U.S.

A push to maritime solutions

So far, when it comes to defense, we’ve seen BBAI traditionally target on the ground solutions and offer AI analytics from anything to drone swarms to predicting potential enemy threats. However, it seems BBAI has made its latest attempt to break into Navy solutions. When it comes to the navy, many small defense players might not have a direct product fitment; their battlefield is, of course, the sea. However, it makes up a very significant portion of the U.S. government's defense spending budget, with the Navy, Air Force, and Army segments all getting a significant share of the pie. For FY25, the Navy/Marine Corps segment has a total resource budget set at about $363 billion. From what I’ve seen, a couple of small defense players have made attempts to adapt their products into something applicable for the Navy. Take Red Cat (RCAT), for example, a small backpack-sized drone player. Recently, we’ve seen their push to a new product, an unmanned surface vessel with much of the same tech we see on their Black Widow drone platform. So seeing BBAI take a similar direction now has made me more bullish.

The latest push in this direction has been the recent partnership with SMX. We saw the announcement at UNITAS 2025. That's the annual multinational naval exercise hosted by the U.S. Navy, specifically the U.S. Naval Forces Southern Command and the 4th Fleet. This is actually one of the longest-running multinational maritime exercises in the world. And it’s all about cooperation deals and readiness among allied and partner nations. So seeing BBAI here, also integrating its ConductorOS system into another platform, is a nice sign. The company is set to partner with SMX to deploy that same AI orchestration and analytics solution, but now for the U.S. Navy’s 4th Fleet. It will, of course, support complex maritime operations. And now across a completely different dynamic environment. BBAI will also integrate its Arcas system. As a reminder, this system provides things like computer vision, pattern-of-life analysis on certain targets, and also risk forecasting. This tech is designed to go on unmanned vehicles and hybrid fleets to deliver something called a unified Common Operating Picture. Essentially, I see the main use here being the detection of unauthorized vessels or suspicious activity in real time. So with all the shipping movements, the Arcas system's job is to take that data and identify what seems out of place. This move also fits in nicely with BBAI’s homeland security connection; the AI system will also be able to identify vehicles/individuals attempting illegal crossings. And with the CEO, Kevin McAleenan, having such close connections to homeland security, being the former head, I see nice tailwinds here in terms of potential contract availability.

Valuation

BBAI shares are currently trading in the low $9 range, with a market cap of $3.27 billion. They also hold a pretty strong cash position at $390.85 million and a total debt figure of $112.64 million. It puts an enterprise value of approximately $2.99 billion on the company. From a valuation perspective, the company appears highly elevated relative to sector norms. The stock has also taken on a lot of momentum this quarter, which leaves investors wondering if there is any room left to run for this stock. Shares dropped into the high $4 range back in mid-September and have since more than doubled. Its forward EV to sales ratio stands at 22.38, and its forward price to sales ratio is 24.46, both are dramatically higher than the sector medians of 3.48 and 3.45, respectively. So me like a lot of other investors, have expectations for significant future growth. Elsewhere, the price to book ratio sits at 12.20 versus a sector median of just 3.84, and the negative historical EPS growth also adds to the speculative nature of the stock. So even though from a product standpoint the company is doing everything right in terms of AI battlefield integration, investors are pricing in considerable execution/market risk.

Areas of concern

It seems BBAI’s defense offering portfolio is rounding out nicely; however, there are a few things we have to keep in mind. When it comes to defense, a lot of smaller names have taken up traction in recent times as a result of the Russia/Ukraine war. The U.S. has allocated lots of spending in that direction, and NATO allies have also been forced to modernize some of their tech after what they have seen in terms of the tech on the battlefield in Ukraine. So operationally, BBAI still remains highly dependent on U.S. Army contracts. These contracts have seen delays, as in the last earnings, and have created some revenue volatility. As we just saw, President Trump has made a big effort to bring a peace deal to the Middle East conflict and has hinted at new efforts to calm tensions in Ukraine. Some may view this as a defense spending pullback in that area. However, even if a ceasefire occurs, NATO and U.S. defense budgets are likely to remain pretty robust, in my opinion. The U.S., in particular Pete Hegseth, has publicly announced its push for a military modernization effort. AI and counter-jamming technologies still have a long way to go in terms of preparing for future contingencies. So I don’t see a spending pullback anytime soon, as we’re only starting to see what AI can do when it comes to defense tech.

What to Watch for 3Q25 Earnings

BBAI’s 3Q25 earnings should arrive in November. Now, investors should focus on a few key metrics to gauge whether the company is actually executing on my growth thesis. First and foremost, those revenue trends will be critical, especially if we see any signs of stabilization or recovery in U.S. Army-related contracts that caused 2Q25 declines. We may see revenue from those delayed contracts show up here. Margins will also be important. BBAI has been ramping up R&D in recent times, and we need to get a sense of how well they are managing those costs and new tech deployments. I’d like to see BBAI’s gross margins rebound from the 25% we saw in 2Q25. Given the transitional phase now within AI and defense, with a ramp-up of higher-value defense contracts, including that edge computing/maritime deployments push, a target range around 27% to 30% gross margin would signal improving cost absorption for me. We should also get an update on progress on Tsecond and SMX from management. So, in terms of how well the two technologies are integrating. Cash burn versus cash balance will also be a big talking point.

data.title: White Brook Capital Partners Q3 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Macro Commentary

The US economy remains resilient. Growth has exceeded expectations, driven by spending from high-end consumers and significant capital expenditures in artificial intelligence. Without the AI investment, growth would likely be stagnant. Labor markets, however, are softening, creating room for potential cuts to short-term interest rates for the remainder of this year.

Announced artificial intelligence ecosystem deals accelerated in the third quarter with a round of long term deals with unknown, and likely conditional terms. In some cases, we view the deals as “money good”, where both sides can satisfy the order in cash; in others these deals are less likely to be economically resolved without significant equity issuances. In general, we find the warrants and shares issued to customers to be problematic as there are scenarios where Nvidia (NVDA) will own appreciable amounts of AMD (AMD) through a stake in OpenAI (OPENAI), should deals consummate as envisioned. We believe a mixture of substance and hype has created a bubble in AI related expenditures. While some liken today’s expenditures to the spend on fiber optic cable two and half decades ago - which was 95% unused by 2007, but is useful and rapidly filling today. Fiber optic cable has a lifespan of 25-40 years, while a silicon processor’s obsolescence is estimated and measured in months - 12, 24, 36. In my view, the problem is more severe today than it was then.

Importantly, there’s a lot of spending across sectors behind AI demand. Everything from electricity and associated generation technologies, to water treatment and pipes have become more expensive to deal with data center cooling needs. In many cases, the associated companies are enjoying stock prices that reflect an exceptionally bullish possible reality. Companies in quantum computing, for example, which is multiple generations of computing standards away, if it's economically feasible at all, and if it is, may not be appropriate for artificial intelligence use cases, and if it is, may not be better than alternative approaches - trade at elevated multiples of forward revenue estimates. For example, Rigetti Computing, Inc (RGTI) is generating a whopping $3 million dollars of quarterly sales and over $15 million of quarterly loss, but enjoying an $18 billion market capitalization. That’s thousands of multiple turns of forward revenue. Calling a bubble is easier today in certain segments than it has ever been.

Positively, capital markets are open again, a boon for small capitalization stocks - while bank executives are also bullish on investment banking activity. Attempts to mainstream less regulated cryptocurrencies continue, as do efforts to move stock trading to less regulated exchanges and markets. We have observed, and expect to continue observing, “unusual” trading activity around major events, which we view as a negative market development. We hope that smaller capitalization stocks can take advantage of the easier lending standards while calmer or at least more honest heads prevail around regulation.

The consumer is strong overall, powered by high income households that have driven almost 100% of discretionary spending growth. There are only conflicting signs of that trend declining. The housing market remains near its lows with mortgage rates above 7% slowing transactions. A recent move closer to 6.5% appears to have been met with increased activity, but we have seen similar brief rallies fail over the past 18 months.

Overall we have been impressed by the relative power of narrative and recent price action over intrinsic value in several sectors of the market and are cautious and selective buyers of individual stocks. Companies that generate actual and significant cash flow have had trouble “catching a bid” - a story that has been true for a long time now. This is not a recommendation to stay out of the market, as the possibility of being wrong is significant and “time in market” usually beats “timing the market”, but to do so with eyes wide open.

The Ethical S&P 500 Strategy

The Ethical S&P 500 Strategy officially started on April 16th. Pro forma year to date, it was up 21.48% through Sept 30th vs 24.63% for the S&P 500. As mentioned earlier, the strategy notably does not include defense companies or companies who customize their goods and services for defense and vice companies.

The Index is extremely diversified and is composed of the same companies as the S&P 500 and with similar concentrations - excepting the exclusions. It is meant as an S&P 500 index replacement for those that want passive exposure to the US’s largest companies but do not want to spread death and destruction. We have been pleased with the strategy's performance and gratified by the interest it has received. The end of the assault in Gaza and renewed focus on ending the war in Ukraine may benefit the index relative to the S&P 500 if military spending, or the prospect of its continuation, declines.

As an example of the work we do for the Ethical S&P 500 strategy - Microsoft (MSFT) is a core holding of other “ethical” indices. We believe the company’s behavior over the past two years disqualifies it from ownership by any investor who doesn’t wish to profit from war. The write-up and the strategy fact sheet is available on White Brook Capital’s website at https://www.whitebrookcapital.com/strategies. The Ethical S&P 500 strategy is now accessible through the Schwab Manager Marketplace and therefore is accessible to any investment manager who custodies at Schwab.

Small & Micro Cap

The Small Cap Absolute Growth Strategy model portfolio, which is a long only strategy that buys small and micro cap stocks with significant growth potential, has had tremendous success in its first year, vastly exceeding expectations. Given their smaller size, the portfolio's companies are less predictable quarter-to-quarter and thus more prone to surprises. However, for the diligent and patient investor, we believe they are also more analyzable, and offer the potential for high returns. Due to the early and significant success of one of the portfolio’s first holdings, the model portfolio is concentrated in that company. Continued success is expected in the near term and therefore we have not materially sold down the position. Over time, the portfolio will become more balanced.

Rapid Micro Biosystems, Inc (RPID) has paced gains, up nearly 300% year to date, but other positions have also significantly contributed to the year to date performance. Paysight, Inc (PAYS), a company no longer in the portfolio, was up over 150% from its entry price. Current positions, Infusystems, Inc (INFU) is up 20%, and CareCloud’s Convertible Preferred (CCLDO) is also up over 20% but the strike of the convertible is at a much lower price than it trades today, and it is therefore also positioned for more, significant gains. Year to date losses are largest in Jakks Pacific, Inc (JAKK), and BranchOut Foods, Inc (BOF) with de minimis losses in Civeo Corporation (CVEO), and no longer held Quipt Home Medical Corp (QIPT) and American Shared Hospital Services (AMS).

Pursuant to the quarter's close, Tile Shop Holdings (TTSH) proposed delisting its shares at a price higher than the close at quarter end. The stated rationale is that the company is not getting credit for its assets as a public company. We agree with the rationale, but find the proposed price to be significantly below our estimate of the company's intrinsic value. We believe that the public needs to see more home building and renovation activity to realize that value. We sold into the news and hope to re-enter if the delisting maneuver is defeated.

The small and micro cap strategy is only open for accredited investors. Due to our focus on price and the lower than average liquidity in the underlying positions, it should be expected to take some time for new accounts to mimic the model portfolio. The CareCloud opportunity for instance is extremely limited, with only the fewest of shares routinely traded now - while the position was relatively easy to amass below $19. Sometime next year, as the number of accounts invested alongside the model portfolio achieves critical mass, we expect to adopt GIPS compliant reporting procedures and then report performance of this strategy more widely.

The All Cap Portfolio

The performance of value oriented stocks continues to be disappointing. While the All-Cap Portfolio bought positions in growth companies as it shifted from mid to an all-cap portfolio, it continues to be overweight value and performance has trailed. Positions in Alphabet, Inc, (GOOG), Nvidia, Inc (NVDA), and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company Limited (TSM) lead the portfolio while Portillo's (PTLO), Builders FirstSource (BLDR), and Floor & Decor (FND) detracted from performance. We still maintain our belief in the thesis that the country is underhoused. We believe that as mortgage rates decline further or stay at a sub 6.5% average interest rate on the 30-year fixed mortgage, the number of new home builds and home sales will increase with corresponding benefits to home building supply companies that supply the materials for new construction and remodel activity.

Portillo's and the fourth largest loser, Target, Inc (TGT) both have new chief executives leading the companies forward while producing significant operating cash flow. At Portillo's, while we supported Michael Asanloo’s strategic vision, the pace of execution under his leadership was slow, prompting a change from the board. White Brook’s thesis, available on the WBC website is still intact with the steps for significant improvement largely the same. That was the problem. We expect the company, which has now stated a desire to pursue the most meaningful parts of that thesis, to execute on them with urgency. Board member, and former CEO of Darden Restaurants has assumed the Chief Executive role, at least temporarily, and we believe has the credibility to make changes to the financing and operations strategy to make Portillo's a successful investment. Target’s choice of CEO was more difficult for us to understand, even while the company generates significant cash flow that it can use to return to shareholders and execute an improved retail strategy. We plan to give the new CEO a quarter to outline his plans for the Company and earn investor trust.

As always, feel free to reach out to discuss this or any of your investments at White Brook Capital. I thank you for your support and will strive to continue to earn your trust.

Sincerely,

data.title: BGB: Discount Worth Watching With Term Approaching
data.description:
Written by Nick Ackerman, co-produced by Stanford Chemist

Earlier this year, we had the opportunity to buy Blackstone Strategic Credit 2027 Term Fund (NYSE:BGB) due to a widening discount. However, we then didn't have to wait too long before selling out of the position as the fund's discount narrowed, with its share price going to almost parity with its net asset value per share. Trades don't always work out so nicely in such a short period of time, but with BGB's anticipated liquidation date coming up in September 2027, this is a name worth keeping a closer eye on.

In particular, the fund has seen its discount widening back out more recently. There have been some credit issues, such as First Brands announcing a bankruptcy—a rather large issuer that has sent some ripples through credit-sensitive investments. In this case, there is some suspicion of fraud, which will be nearly impossible to account for by management teams that make investing choices.

BGB Basics

  • 1-Year Z-score: -0.62
  • Discount/Premium: -4.87%
  • Distribution Yield: 7.86%
  • Expense Ratio: 2.06%
  • Leverage: 36.8%
  • Managed Assets: $885 million
  • Structure: Term (anticipated liquidation date Sept. 15, 2027).

BGB has an investment objective to "seek high current income, with a secondary objective to seek preservation of capital, consistent with its primary goal of high current income."

To achieve this, the fund will "invest primarily in a diversified portfolio of loans and other fixed-income instruments predominantly US Corporate issuers, including first- and second-lien loans and high yield corporate bonds of varying maturities."

When including the fund's leverage expenses, the total expense ratio comes to 4.81%. The fund is also fairly highly leveraged, which has been positive in the last several years for the fund, but creates greater volatility and risk of downside moves as well.

Term Structure Opportunity

When investing in CEFs, relative historical discount levels can be one of the key factors when considering whether a fund looks like a good value or not. There are always exceptions, such as fundamental changes or distribution changes that can change what might be an appropriate discount/premium.

Additionally, another exception is for term-structured funds that have potential liquidation dates. These are "potential" terminations because management and the Board often have the choice to extend the term date, or potentially go perpetual as well.

In the case of BGB, we are trading below the fund's historical discount level, but we are getting fairly near the liquidation date in just under two years. That can put something of a floor on how far the discount might go.

As of today, the alpha comes to 2.54% based on the 1.92 years of life left. Of course, this doesn't guarantee that positive total returns can be generated, but it does help to provide alpha. The alpha would be based on a fund that was invested the same, but without a term structure—meaning the discount wouldn't be realized compared to BGB.

For BGB, they can extend the life of the fund by "prior to the date of dissolution, a majority of the Board of Trustees, with the approval of a majority of the outstanding voting securities entitled to a vote, may extend the life of the Fund." This can occur by a period of "two years or such shorter time as may be determined." They also allow that the term can be extended an unlimited number of times.

Essentially, that could make it a perpetual fund. Funds can also bring a vote to shareholders to eliminate the term structure and switch it to a perpetual fund. That has occurred on a couple of funds previously, but those were funds that consistently traded at premiums before the elimination of the term structure.

Most term-structured funds also allow for switching to a perpetual fund after a tender offer of 100% of outstanding shares at 100% of NAV. BGB came to the market at a time when that wasn't common wording in the prospectus, but that is once again something that the management could attempt. The tender offer would mean that investors can still participate in the complete liquidation of their position, but those who want to continue to hold are free to do so.

Finally, with 10% of its portfolio listed in Level 3 securities, this presents another potential uncertainty. These valuations are subject to best 'guesses' rather than actual value compared to securities that offer greater liquidity. This has caused some issues for some funds that we've seen liquidate with Invesco and, more recently, with Amundi (Pioneer) funds.

...Pioneer Diversified High Income Fund (HNW) and Pioneer High Income Fund (PHT) that ceased trading at the close of September 25, 2025, we received a troubling announcement when the liquidating distributions of both funds fell substantially below their last stated NAVs:

BGB's Distribution, Performance And First Brand Exposure

The performance of the fund has been fairly strong on a YTD basis and for the last couple of years now. In a higher-rate environment, the fund benefited from increased total investment income, leading to higher net investment income and a payout that was trending higher.

However, with the Fed now cutting rates—first 100 basis points in late 2024 and now another 25 in September 2025, with more projected—that is sending the payout lower for this fund and other interest rate sensitive funds like BGB.

This is primarily a floating rate-based portfolio with ~80% invested in senior secured loans and a smaller portion listed in high-yield bonds. As is expected in this type of portfolio, the overall credit quality here is below-investment grade at 99% of the exposure. This composition means low duration, at just 0.73 years.

The latest distribution rate comes in at 7.86%, with a NAV rate of 7.48%. The fund's credit facility leverage costs should also decline some as the Fed cuts its benchmark rate. However, they also have Mandatory Redeemable Preferred shares with a fixed 6.6% dividend rate. These are redeemable on July 25, 2027, shortly before the fund's own anticipated liquidation date.

With all that said, NII is still likely to decrease overall, and further distribution trims would be expected.

While the distribution may be under pressure, reversing the increases we saw previously, total returns can still be attractive.

Against a couple of its peers, BGB has seen relatively stronger total NAV returns in the last few years. The total share price results were a bit weaker compared to BlackRock Floating Rate Income Strategies Fund (FRA), but that has to do with the discount/premium gyrations.

The whole space has done fairly well overall, as measured by the Invesco Senior Loan ETF (BKLN). I've also included the iShares iBoxx High-Yield Corporate Bond ETF (HYG) due to the sleeve that BGB carries. The leverage in the closed-end funds BGB, FRA and Invesco Senior Income Trust (VVR) would have contributed to better total returns here during this stronger period.

More recently, we have seen the share price of BGB heading lower, and its NAV has also been taking a move lower now in the last month or so.

As of the last semi-annual report, for the period ended June 30, 2025, BGB held two positions in First Brands Group.

In the full holdings list as of Aug. 31, 2025, they still noted having these two positions at a whopping weight of 0.25% and 0.17%. This is out of nearly 600 total holdings as well—meaning that the total exposure here is not really all that much to panic over. The leverage means that the losses are amplified, but the significant diversification should still keep it rather minimal.

Conclusion

BGB's term structure could provide some opportunity, as the liquidation could come in less than two years. That can make BGB worth keeping a closer eye on for tactical investors. The current discount isn't a screaming buy, but there is likely to be some floor here moving forward. On the other hand, the term could be extended or eliminated with shareholder approval. That could dramatically reduce the potential opportunity for alpha. The fund will be subject to credit risks in a potentially deteriorating economy, all amplified by the leverage the fund employs.

data.title: J.B. Hunt: I Would Sell Into Strength After Q3 Results (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
I wrote an article about J.B. Hunt Transport Services, Inc. (NASDAQ:JBHT) back in June 2025. At the time, JBHT stock was trading for about $145 per share, and I gave it a Hold rating. Since then, it has traded in a range of between around $130 to $156 per share, (prior to Q3 earnings results). However, it recently jumped after the company reported Q3 earnings, so I wanted to take another look.

The Chart

As the chart below shows, this stock has surged after Q3 results with a gain of nearly 20%. It now trades for about $166 per share, which is well above the 50-day moving average of $140.46 and the 200-day which is $147.83. This stock is overbought, and that is one reason why I would use this opportunity to sell into strength.

Q3 Earnings Report

For the 3rd quarter of 2025, J.B. Hunt Transport Services reported GAAP earnings of $1.76 per share, which beat estimates of $1.46 per share. Revenues came in at $3.05 billion, which also beat by $30 million. For the sake of comparisons, in Q3 2024, the company earned $1.49 per share and had revenues of $3.07 billion. I think this is a case of very low expectations being beaten, and part of this was due to management doing a great job at controlling costs in a weak environment for the freight industry. One aspect that seems to have helped is that truckload capacity is diminishing in this industry because there has been an ongoing freight recession, however, the overall demand remains weak, as noted in these comments by management during the call:

"truckload capacity continued to exit the market and the pace of exits is accelerating, but the soft demand environment is likely muting the market impact of capacity attrition."

Some of the positives from the earnings release include the fact that the company reported it repurchased about 5.4 million shares or $780 million year-to-date. It also said it was continuing to make progress with a $100 million cost reduction program, which is expected to be more fully realized in 2026.

The stock jumped after the earnings results came out, but I believe this is most likely a short-term bounce that is probably not sustainable. The economy appears to be slowing down and tariff issues and a trade war with China are likely to continue impacting the economy into 2026.

Earnings Estimates And The Balance Sheet

The earnings estimates below have been provided by Seeking Alpha, and indicate a price to earnings ratio of about 25 times for 2025. Earnings are expected to rise in the coming years which could bring the PE ratio down slightly, but I still feel the valuation is rich going forward for a cyclical and low growth industry.

As for the balance sheet, this company has about $2.04 billion in debt, and nearly $51 million in cash. I view this balance sheet as another reason for this stock to trade with a lower PE ratio because the cash level is dwarfed by the debt load. In a prolonged recession, this could become a real concern for investors.

The Dividend

This company pays a dividend of $0.44 per share each quarter, which totals $1.76 per share annually. This provides a yield of about 1.25%.

Why I Would Use This As An Opportunity To Sell Into Strength

According to Shortsqueeze.com, there are about 4 million shares currently short, and this represents nearly 3 days worth of trading volume. I believe the shorts were temporarily caught off-guard by the stronger than expected earnings results. However, I think they will reassert selling pressure on this stock in the days ahead because better than expected results primarily due to cost controls will not change the ongoing concerns.

I do not view the freight industry as being an attractive long-term investment. It is a capital intensive, low-growth and cyclical business that is also highly dependent on the economy as well as fuel and labor costs. As such, I believe there are too many potential risk factors and I also think investing in this stock and the freight industry comes with a major opportunity cost. When you can invest in businesses that are less cyclical and have significant growth and cash-rich balance sheets, it makes taking risks in the freight industry less appealing. While the stock reaction has been very positive towards Q3 earnings, I believe this will be short-lived because of valuation and because the ongoing challenges for the freight industry are ongoing. Some of the headwinds include these issues:

  1. Consumer spending is nearly 2/3rds of the U.S. economy and there are major signs that the consumer is under financial pressure right now. Major cracks in the jobs market and other issues with consumers have started to appear and this could be a warning sign for the economy going forward. There has been a surge in delinquencies in all sorts of consumer loans ranging from auto loans, credit cards and student loans and there has even been a significant rise in searches for "help with mortgage", which just hit the same level as they did in 2009. This is a troubling sign and deteriorating consumer sentiment can impact spending and that could reduce freight volumes in the future.
  2. The tariff situation remains very uncertain and the trade war with China seems to have taken a turn for the worse. President Trump recently said he would put an additional 100% tariff on China, which is due to start on November 1. As I said in my last article on this company, some industries seem to have resorted to stockpiling for certain inventory items and consumers may have also stockpiled in advance of expected tariff price increases. This may have created a pull-forward of demand in Q3, which could result in lower demand going forward. Management at J.B. Hunt Transport has said tariffs could impact the company, and it is clear that tariffs are an ongoing risk factor for the freight industry and the U.S. economy in general.

Where I Could Be Wrong

The Fed recently cut interest rates, and Jerome Powell is expected to announce more rate cuts later this year. Some investors believe that the Fed can pull off a soft-landing and avoid a recession. If they succeed, there is a chance that this stock has already bottomed out and that the jobs market will stabilize. The AI capex spending boom could also drive economic growth that helps the U.S. economy from going into recession.

In Summary

The better than expected Q3 earnings results seem to be primarily driven by cost controls rather than growth or a big change in the fundamentals or the economy. I believe the freight industry remains challenging and at risk of tariffs and a weakening consumer. For these reasons, I believe the surge in the stock price is an opportunity to sell into strength.

data.title: The Coming Mortality Cliff
data.description:
Back in the late 1990s, GDP growth was north of four percent for four straight years from 1996-1999. It was the last time the United States enjoyed that rate of sustained growth. There were several reasons behind that robust level of growth, that has not been replicated since.

The country and the world were in the midst of a technological paradigm shift with the birth of internet. Much like the current AI Revolution, tech spending was surging and contributing greatly to GDP growth. In addition, China was not yet part of the WTO and the Cold War had been won after a half century of containment and sacrifice.

The U.S. was the unquestioned leader of a multipolar world and owner of the global reserve currency. And being the unquestioned hegemon tends to have significant investment advantages. After all, during the time of the Caesars, where did you want to invest? In Rome or Gaul? After the defeat of the Spanish Armada in the late 1500s, where would you position your portfolio? London or Spain?

The other key economic driver in the late 1990s was demographics, which as the saying goes, is destiny. Baby Boomers were in their prime earning years and funneling a good chunk of those wages into their 401Ks. The median age in the U.S. was 34 years old in 1999. It is now 38.3 years. Productivity gains averaged nearly 3.5% annually from 1998-2000. The productivity rate from 2021-2024 averaged just over 1.25%.

The labor participation rate in 1999 was 66.4%. It currently averages just over 62%. This is one reason the U.S. debt and deficit have exploded this century as there is a smaller percentage of people working and more citizens that are on taxpayer funded benefits like Social Security and Medicare. Wars in Iraq, Afghanistan and other conflicts in the Middle East as well as new entitlements like Obamacare also have been key drivers of the deteriorating U.S. fiscal situation.

Clearly current demographic trends are not nearly as favorable during the Internet Boom. And yet, markets are trading at valuation levels not seen since just before the Internet Bust in early 2000.

So, let's talk about how demographics continuing to deteriorate will impact the U.S. economy in the years ahead. Let's start with U.S. residential real estate, the largest asset class in the world. Baby Boomers held approximately half of all home equity in the United States as of 2024. This equates to some $17 trillion in home equity and nearly $20 trillion of wealth overall.

The oldest Baby Boomer will hit 80 years old in 2026. Approximately 10,000 Baby Boomers are passing daily now. A rate that will increase in the coming years as this generational cohort continues to age. Many will enter nursing and assisted living homes. Think of all the homes that are going to come on the market to settle inheritances and/or provide funding for their later years for increased medical costs.

This comes at a time when the U.S. housing market is already struggling mightily. Home sellers outnumber home buyers by the largest amount in more than a decade and 2026 is shaping up to be a brutal year in housing. Many metro regions like Austin have already seen more than a 20% drop in average home prices since they peaked.

Housing activity accounts for 15% to 18% of economic history in the United States. In addition, outside jobs losses, few things more negatively impact consumer sentiment than a deteriorating housing market and falling home prices. And consumer sentiment is already historically low.

While probably not as meaningful as to the real estate market, the aging Baby Boomers will impact the stock market in the coming years as well. Obviously, stock portfolios are easier to transfer and divvy up to settle inheritances than primary residences. However, as people age, they tend to move their portfolios onto a more conservative stance (more bonds, less stocks), which is a marginal negative for equities.

The U.S. birth rate has declined to just 1.64 births per woman. This is half the rate of the 1950s. While not as low as many countries in Europe, it is far below the replacement rate of 2.1 births per woman.

With the mass re-migration currently ongoing in the country in 2025 which has seen more than two million recent migrants leaving the U.S. year to date, this year might go down as the first time in American history where its population has shrunk.

In summary, investors are currently focused on a potential AI bubble, increasing tariff impacts, a faltering jobs market, sticky inflation, Federal Reserve actions, trade tensions with China, etc. However, over the long-term few things will impact economic growth, productivity, the real estate market, the fiscal situation, etc...more than the coming mortality cliff.

This is continuing to develop even as equities trade at extreme levels viewed via many historical valuation measures. I leave with this corollary. The Japanese Nikkei peaked in 1989 at the time when the median age in Japan was just under 37 years old. By the time the Nikkei regained that peak 35 years later, the median age in the country was 11 years higher. Food for thought.

data.title: Sana Biotechnology: Scope Goes Beyond HIP-Modified Pancreatic Islet Cells For T1D
data.description:
The last time I wrote an article on Sana Biotechnology, Inc. (NASDAQ:SANA), it was in a Seeking Alpha article entitled "Sana: HIP-Modified Islet Cells To Possibly Change Scope Of Type 1 Diabetes Treatment." With respect to this article, I noted the company's opportunity to change how such patients with Type 1 Diabetes [T1D] are treated. I'm happy to state that the company is still making progress on this front, and this is based on the publication of 12-week results from an investigator-sponsored trial in one patient who showed that they were able to produce insulin in a dose-dependent manner while at the same time avoiding immune rejection from an allogeneic or autologous cell therapy. Beyond the scope of 12 weeks, even better, this noted effect was shown to last over an extended period of 6 months.

The point is that the company's Hypoimmune [HIP] platform is moving things forward, as islet cells are known to provide the ability to generate insulin for patients, but the major problem at the moment is that they must take frequent, lifelong toxic immunosuppressive therapy in order to accomplish this goal. With this newly released data, the company hopes to change the treatment paradigm for these patients with T1D.

With that being said, a catalyst to watch with respect to this program is the filing of an IND for SC451 as a next-generation HIP-modified pancreatic islet cell therapy for these patients in 2026. This program is moving forward, but this time around I want to go over another candidate that the company is in the process of evaluating. That is, it is developing a HIP-modified CD19-directed allogeneic CAR T-cell therapy, SC291, to treat patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders. Specifically, the goal is to explore the use of this therapy to target patients with refractory systemic lupus erythematosus [SLE] and ANCA-associated vasculitis [AAV] in the phase 1 GLEAM trial. The reason why I believe it is important to go over this study is because Sana expects to release data from this early-stage study in 2025.

In addition, it also expects to share results from the ongoing phase 1 VIVID study using HIP-modified CD22-directed allogeneic CAR T-cell therapy to target patients with relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies. In particular, these are patients who have already received prior CD19-directed CAR T therapy. The last time around I rated this stock a "Strong Buy" and I'm going to keep to this rating because of further confirmation it obtained from the one patient who was given primary pancreatic islet cell therapy UP421 was shown to continue to generate insulin for the patient and not require immunosuppression over a six-month period. That, plus the fact that there are two separate data readouts expected in 2025. One is from the program of SC291 to treat patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders, and then the other program involves SC262 to treat patients with B-cell malignancies.

B-Cell Depletion Plays An Important Role For Autoimmune Disorders, And HIP-Modified CD19 Cell Therapy Could Boost This Role

As I noted above, the company is doing well in terms of developing its pancreatic islet cell therapy development program targeting patients with Type 1 Diabetes [T1D]. However, for the purpose of this article, I specifically want to go over its program involving the use of B-cell depletion to treat patients with certain types of B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders. This would be in terms of the company's ongoing phase 1 GLEAM trial using SC291 as a HIP-modified CD19-directed allogeneic CAR T-cell therapy to target such patients.

What I'm going to go over below are the three separate cohorts from the ongoing GLEAM study that each recruit different types of autoimmune disorder patients. That is, the main goal of this early-stage trial is to go after the following B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders as follows:

  • Lupus Nephritis [LN].
  • Extrarenal systemic lupus erythematosus [ERL].
  • Anti-neutrophil cytoplasmic antibody [ANCA]-associated vasculitis [AAV].

Before going over this ongoing early-stage study in detail, plus any catalysts to arise from this program, I believe it is important to go over what each of such disorders is and what the possible market opportunity for each could end up being for the company. Systemic lupus erythematosus [SLE], or lupus, is characterized as an autoimmune disorder in which the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues and organs. When there is specific renal [kidney] involvement, this disorder is known as lupus nephritis [LN]. However, the lupus disorder itself isn't just confined to only attacking the kidneys. What it could also have an impact on would be other parts of a person's body, like the skin, brain, and/or joints.

Speaking of which, all of these patients are plagued by the symptom of joint pain at some point in the disease. In terms of ANCA-AAV, it is a rare type of autoimmune disorder that causes inflammation to come about in the blood vessels of a patient, which leads to a host of symptoms. The global systemic lupus erythematosus market is expected to reach $4.26 billion by 2030. This is a very large market indeed, and the hope is that the company will be able to capture a large chunk of it.

In order to see whether or not SC291 as a HIP-modified allogeneic CD19-directed CAR T therapy will be able to treat these patients with refractory SLE disease, the company deployed the ongoing phase 1 GLEAM study. The good thing about this study is that it is able to be a dose-finding one. Meaning that there is dose escalation in place to see what type of doses the company could elevate to. This is crucial, because in the prior ARDENT study it was noted that there was a dose-dependent depletion of B cells [Sana Corporate Presentation August 2025, slide #25]. In essence, the higher the dose, the better the B-cell depletion. Thus, going to the highest doses possible might be the idea to achieve desired efficacy. However, one important item to point out is that such B-cell depletion was demonstrated in B-cell cancer patients. It remains to be seen if this can be replicated in these patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders instead.

Speaking of this prior ARDENT study, it was noted that there were no cases of Grade 2 or higher cytokine release syndrome [CRS] and no Immune Effector Cell-Associated Neurotoxicity Syndrome [ICANS]. The way that this study is done is to give patients a single dose of SC291 who are given preconditioning with agents cyclophosphamide [CY] and fludarabine [FLU]. It is important to state that SLE itself is subcategorized into two specific indication types, which are:

  • Lupus Nephritis - SLE with kidney involvement.
  • Extrarenal lupus - SLE that affects all other organs other than the kidney.

These two indications make up two of the cohorts being explored, with the 3rd cohort being ANCA-AAV. It is important to point out that this is the direction that Sana chose to go forward with first. Should it release positive data from the GLEAM trial for these indications, then it could potentially expand to other autoimmune disorder indications. Believe it or not, there is a lot that it could expand to. As a matter of fact, there are >75 B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders it could go after [Sana Corporate Presentation, August 2025, slide #22].

This is where a catalyst opportunity comes into play for this company, which is that it expects to release data from the phase 1 GLEAM trial in 2025. Then, as I mentioned above in the beginning, it intends to also release data from the phase 1 VIVID trial using its HIP-modified allogeneic CD22-directed CAR T-cell therapy to target patients with r/r B-cell malignancies. Again, with the emphasis on recruiting patients who had already received prior CD19-directed CAR-T therapy.

Autologous CAR Ts have done well to target patients with B-cell-mediated disorders, but there are disadvantages to using them, such as difficult-to-scale manufacturing and a long gap before a patient can be treated with the intended cell therapy. This is where the company's HIP-modified allogeneic CD19-directed CAR T therapy can be far more helpful. There is no need to perform apheresis [cell donor therapy off the shelf given], off the shelf [no need to wait four weeks to give a patient treatment], and can be scalable in large quantities [no limited supply].

Financials

According to the 10-Q SEC Filing, Sana Biotechnology had cash, cash equivalents, and marketable securities of $72.7 million as of June 30, 2025. Of course, it is important to note that this does not at all include the cash that the company raised afterwards. One financial tool used was the sale of common stock through its ATM in July and August of 2025, whereby it raised total gross proceeds of $29.5 million. In addition to the cash it raised through an underwritten public offering, where it raised $75 million. This ended up being around $100 million in cash it raised with these other financing events.

With the cash it had beforehand of $72.7 million, plus the approximately $105 million it raised through these other transactions, SANA believes that it has enough to fund its operations into the 2nd half of 2026. It should have a decent amount of cash for now, but the truth is that it is likely going to need to raise additional funds soon, at least IMO, at the latest in the 1st half of 2026. Thus, be aware of a dilution risk, as the company may likely seek to raise funds earlier than anticipated.

Risks To Business

There are several risks that investors should be aware of before investing in Sana Biotechnology. The first risk to go over would be in terms of the data observed thus far with primary islet cell therapy UP421 treating the one patient with T1D. The risk here is that the ability to give a patient the ability to generate their own insulin, without the need for immunosuppression over a 6-month period, was only proven in 1 patient. There is no assurance that other patients given this therapy are going to also have insulin-producing cells such as this. Plus, the candidate that the company is moving forward with is SC451. An IND filing to begin a phase 1 study for this program targeting patients with T1D is expected in 2026. The risk with this is that there is no assurance that the IND filing will be accepted by the FDA, nor that Sana will receive a go-ahead to initiate a phase 1 study using SC451 to treat these patients with T1D.

The second risk to consider would be in terms of the two expansion opportunities I mentioned above, which are the development of SC291 in the ongoing phase 1 GLEAM trial targeting patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders and then SC262 being explored in the ongoing phase 1 VIVID trial targeting patients with relapsed/refractory B-cell malignancies. Data from both of these trials are expected during this year, 2025, and there is no guarantee that the data from one or both of these studies is going to end up being positive. Nor that the clinical results produced from them are going to be enough to warrant further investigation of these indications in phase 2 studies. Failure for SC291 or SC262 to gain traction as programs would mean that the company would be reliant on preclinical development of SC451 for T1D.

The third and final risk to consider would be the financial position that this company is in. The reason why I state that is because even with the few cash raises it performed this year, it still has a cash projection runway of the 2nd half of 2026. This means that at the latest, it would have to consider raising cash in the 1st half of 2026, which is rapidly approaching. Not only at this point, but also where it could choose to raise additional funds to keep itself funded would be with the upcoming data releases. As I noted above, it is gearing up to release data from both ongoing phase 1 GLEAM and VIVID studies, targeting patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders and B-cell-mediated malignancies, respectively. If positive data is achieved for either of these studies, causing the stock price to trade significantly higher, then management may not hesitate to pull the trigger and raise cash on the back of a huge share price increase.

Conclusion

Sana Biotechnology continues to show that its HIP-modified pancreatic islet cell therapy can achieve a durable outcome for 1 patient in being able to generate their own insulin without the need for any type of immunosuppression with it. To this end, it is gearing up to file an IND for its next-generation HIP-modified stem-cell-derived pancreatic islet cell therapy in early 2026 and initiate a phase 1 study shortly thereafter. This is one part of its pipeline; the other portion that is still progressing would be its off-the-shelf allogeneic CAR-T therapies being explored in phase 1 studies targeting patients with B-cell-mediated autoimmune disorders and malignancies. With data from both of these indications expected in 2025, these are other potential shareholder value creation events. I do want to mention that the stock price of Sana traded higher the other day by 24%, after Canadian hedge fund manager Eric Jackson said "that he is long the stock and it could potentially be a 100 bagger".

With a 6-month durable response of insulin being produced by one patient with no immunosuppressive therapy after being given HIP-modified pancreatic islet cell therapy, plus the expected data readouts from both GLEAM and VIVID studies in 2025, I believe that this stock should be kept at a "Strong Buy" rating. Plus, it is in preclinical work to take the targeting of B-cell-mediated disorders even further, and it would be in terms of deploying its fusogen technology platform. The purpose of this technology is to have SG299 deliver to CD8+ T-cells genetic material required to make CD19-directed CAR T-cells, but at the same time avoid it being able to track into liver or gonadal tissue. Even better, to also incorporate the ability to treat patients with no prior lymphodepletion at all. An IND filing for SG299 is expected in early 2026.

data.title: Patient Opportunity Equity Strategy Q3 2025 Portfolio Activity And Attribution
data.description:
By Christina Siegel Malbon

Fund Highlights

During the third quarter of 2025, the Patient Opportunity Equity Strategy generated a total return of 14.1% net of fees. In comparison, the strategy’s unmanaged benchmark, the S&P 500 Index, returned 8.1%.

Using a three-factor performance attribution model, selection and interaction effects contributed to the portfolio’s outperformance which was partially offset by allocation effects. Precigen Convertible Perpetual Preferreds, Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL), Precigen Warrants, Alibaba Group Holdings (BABA) and Precigen Inc. (PGEN) were the largest contributors to performance, while Dave & Buster’s Entertainment (PLAY), QXO Inc. (QXO), Crocs Inc. (CROX), IAC Inc. (IAC) and Mattel Inc. (MAT) were the largest detractors.

Relative to the index, the strategy was overweight the Consumer Discretionary, Communication Services, Financials, Energy, Industrials, and Health Care sectors on average during the quarter. With zero allocation to Real Estate, Utilities, Materials, and Consumer Staples, the portfolio was underweight these sectors along with Information Technology.

The portfolio eliminated five positions, Canada Goose Holdings Inc. (GOOS), Clear Secure Inc. (YOU), Kosmos Energy ltd (KOS), Precigen Convertible Perpetual Preferreds (which were converted into common), and Costco Wholesale Corp. Put Options (COST 1/16/26 P965) during the quarter.

The portfolio ended the quarter with 36 holdings, where the top 10 stocks represented 51.1% of total assets compared to 38.9% for the index, highlighting the portfolio’s meaningful active share of around 92.5%.

Portfolio Review

The market continued its upward trajectory in the third quarter, benefiting from resilient consumer spending, Federal Reserve rate cuts, and sustained AI investments. Our portfolio benefited as long-held positions broke through multi-year resistance overhangs.

As long-term investors, we estimate a company's value five, ten, and occasionally even twenty years into the future. Our goal is to identify businesses where underlying fundamentals diverge meaningfully from market expectations. Our favorite opportunities tend to have durable competitive advantages, large addressable markets, and world-class management teams. Companies with these attributes rarely trade at significant discounts, as their superior attributes are well recognized. However, periods of negative sentiment typically provide our entry point. Years of studying these businesses, understanding their management teams, and knowing how they create value gives us the conviction to build or add to positions during sell-offs.

Alphabet Inc. (GOOGL) and Alibaba Group Holdings (BABA), positions we have held since 2020 and 2019, respectively, led portfolio performance after years of being dismissed as “un-investable”. Alphabet gained regulatory clarity while Alibaba unveiled aggressive AI investment plans. Although sentiment reversals are inherently unpredictable, our focus remains on the divergence between fundamentals and market expectations. In both cases, expectations had stayed low even as fundamentals strengthened, creating value we were willing to wait to realize.

We continue to monetize volatility in both directions. Weakness in travel-related names during the second quarter allowed us to meaningfully increase our positions in Delta Air Lines (DAL) and Norwegian Cruise Line Holdings (NCLH), both of which delivered strong returns in the third quarter. Similarly, we doubled our exposure to UnitedHealth Group (UNH) during this quarter’s pullback, establishing an attractive foundation for long-term returns.

Finally, Precigen (PGEN), the strategy’s top contributor, represents a relationship spanning more than a decade. As you may recall, we led a PIPE (private investment in public entity) transaction that gave us exposure to the company through convertible perpetual preferreds, and warrants. Our thesis was straightforward: the company possessed a valuable near-term asset (Papzimeos) and promising pipeline but needed capital to reach critical milestones. In early August, the FDA approved Papzimeos with a better than anticipated label. Following the ensuing rally, we converted our preferreds into common stock, realizing substantial gains while maintaining significant exposure.

The opportunity remains compelling. Papzimeos is now launching, while earlier-stage pipeline assets continue advancing. CEO Dr. Helen Sabzevari is an outstanding leader with a proven track record of bringing drugs to market. While the risks inherent in drug development remain high, we believe the risk/reward profile remains attractive, with the market still undervaluing the full potential of Papzimeos and the broader pipeline.

New and Eliminated

This quarter we exited five positions in the period, using the proceeds to reallocate capital towards our highest-conviction names. We collected tax losses by exiting Canada Goose Holdings Inc. (GOOS) and Kosmos Energy Ltd (KOS) in the quarter. We converted our Precigen Convertible Perpetual Preferreds into common stock in the period to take advantage of the conversion price. We closed our Costco Wholesale Corp. Put Option (COST 1/16/26 P965) while exiting our small position in Clear Secure Inc. (CLEAR).

Top Contributors and Top Detractors
